card_title: 3 ways CNY actually reaches your supplier

videos.video_title: The 3 paths your CNY takes to China (most operators only know one)

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY routing (internal: World Account + MYbank)
variants.vo: Pattern we see across the operators on our books. Three different paths get used to send CNY to China, and most teams only know about one of them. Path one is SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five days, two or three intermediaries, each one shaving a fee off the wire. Path two is CIPS, China's own clearing system. One to two days, no SWIFT chain, no hidden deductions. Path three is the direct domestic rail used for 1688 suppliers. Same-day arrival, full amount, supplier sees the money before lunch. Same dollar amount leaving your account, three completely different outcomes on the supplier side. The supplier doesn't care which path you used. They care what landed and when. WorldFirst routes CNY on the fast paths by default. Link's in the bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst (internal: World Account)
variants.vo: Quick maths on how your CNY actually reaches your supplier. Path one, SWIFT. Three to five business days. One to three correspondent banks. Each one deducts fifteen to fifty dollars off the wire. On a fifty thousand dollar payment, that's up to a hundred and fifty gone before the supplier sees anything. Path two, CIPS. One to two days. No correspondent chain. No deductions. Path three, the direct rail for 1688 suppliers. Same-day. Full amount lands. Check your last wire confirmation. If the routing line says SWIFT and your supplier got less than you sent, you're on path one paying path-one prices. WorldFirst defaults to the same-day rail. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst (internal: World Account)
variants.vo: Everyone treats sending CNY to China like it's one thing. It isn't. It's three completely different rails, each priced differently, each taking a different number of days. The bank doesn't explain this because they earn the most on the slowest one. SWIFT takes three to five days because the money physically hops between correspondent banks who each take a cut. CIPS takes one to two because China built its own rail that doesn't need SWIFT. The 1688 domestic rail takes hours because it never leaves the Chinese system. Same destination. Same amount of money. Three different worlds. Your bank picked the slow one for you. WorldFirst picks the fast one. Bio.

card_title: Your supplier asked you to pay the $200 they didn't receive

videos.video_title: Your supplier just asked for the $200 they did not get

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay + MYbank)
variants.vo: Pattern we see roughly once a week across the operators we work with. The wire receipt says fifty thousand sent. The WhatsApp from the supplier says forty-nine thousand eight hundred arrived. Two hundred missing, and nobody can explain where it went. Both sides are telling the truth. Here's what actually happened. The wire went through SWIFT. SWIFT doesn't move money. It just passes messages between real banks in the middle, called correspondents. A US to China wire usually touches one to three of them. Each one deducts fifteen to fifty dollars off the wire before passing it on. Your bank never tells you. Your supplier just sees the smaller number land. That two hundred dollar gap isn't a scam. It's the chain. WorldFirst skips the chain entirely on CNY payments. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay)
variants.vo: Quick maths on why your supplier got less than you sent. You wired fifty thousand. Three correspondent banks sat in the SWIFT chain. Bank one deducted fifty dollars. Bank two deducted thirty-five. Bank three deducted eighty. Forty-nine thousand eight hundred and thirty-five landed. Supplier's WhatsApp message: short one hundred and sixty-five, please top up. So you wire again. New send fee, twenty-five dollars. New FX margin, another point five percent on the top-up amount. Total cost of one supplier payment, after correction, climbs past three hundred dollars in fees you didn't see coming. The fix is one of two things. Ask your bank to send as OUR instead of SHA, which costs thirty to seventy-five upfront but means the supplier gets the full amount. Or skip the SWIFT chain entirely. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers without the correspondent hops. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay)
variants.vo: Everyone blames the supplier when the amount comes in short. Truth is, three banks just took a piece of your wire and nobody told you. SWIFT is a messaging system, not a money system. Real banks in the middle pass the funds hop by hop, and each one is legally allowed to deduct its own fee. Fifteen to fifty dollars per hop, one to three hops, on every single China wire. The supplier isn't lying. Your bank isn't lying. The middle is just invisible to both of you. Stop arguing with the supplier. The chain ate the money. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers without the chain. Bio.

videos.video_title: Why your wire arrived 3 days late and short $150

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay)
variants.vo: Pattern we see across China-bound wires. Day two, supplier hasn't received anything. Day five, supplier is still chasing. Day seven, the wire shows up short. Two things are happening in parallel. One, the correspondent chain. A US to China SWIFT wire usually routes through one to three banks. Each one takes a business day to process and shaves fifteen to fifty dollars off the wire. Two, the China-side compliance review. Chinese banks check the beneficiary name on the wire against the registered company name. If the wire says Shenzhen ABC Trading but the registration says Shenzhen ABC Trading Co Ltd, the wire pauses for manual review. Three to ten days. Sometimes returned. Two minutes spent matching the exact registered name saves a week of supplier emails. WorldFirst handles supplier name verification before the wire leaves. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay)
variants.vo: Quick maths on why your wire is late and short. Day one, sender bank releases the funds. Day two, correspondent A holds for processing, deducts fifty. Day three, correspondent B holds, deducts thirty-five. Day four, the Chinese bank holds for compliance review on the beneficiary name. Day five, the supplier finally sees a wire that's a hundred and fifty dollars short. Total cost of one delayed wire. Production timeline pushed back five business days. Supplier asks for a top-up of a hundred and fifty. Another wire, another twenty-five dollar send fee, another FX margin on the top-up. Real cost of a five hundred dollar fee saving on the sender side, easily six hundred plus in correction costs and a week of lost production. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers same-day, full amount, no chain. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay)
variants.vo: Everyone thinks a late wire is the bank's fault. Truth is, half the time it's a five-character mismatch on the company name. Chinese banks legally have to verify the wire's beneficiary name against the registered company name. If your wire says Shenzhen ABC Trading and the registration says Shenzhen ABC Trading Co Ltd, that's a mismatch. Manual review. Three to ten days frozen. Sometimes the wire gets sent back. The supplier blames you. You blame the bank. The actual problem is that nobody verified the exact registered name before the wire went out. WorldFirst verifies the supplier's registered name before the payment runs. Bio.

card_title: Why paying your supplier in CNY actually wins

videos.video_title: USD feels safer. It costs you 2% more.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Pattern we see across operators paying Chinese suppliers in USD. Two FX conversions are baked into the route, and neither one shows up as a fee. Your bank converts your local currency to USD when the wire is created. Point five to one point five percent margin, hidden in the rate. Your supplier's bank converts that USD back into CNY when it lands. Point three to point eight percent margin, also hidden, charged on their side and quietly priced back into your next PI. On a twenty thousand dollar order, that stack quietly costs one hundred and sixty to four hundred and sixty dollars before a single carton ships. Pay in CNY directly, one conversion, point three to point five percent total. Same payment, half the cost, same supplier account. WorldFirst holds CNY natively, that's why this doesn't happen. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Quick maths on USD versus CNY for a twenty thousand dollar supplier payment. USD route. Your bank's FX margin, one percent, that's two hundred dollars. Supplier's bank converts USD to CNY at point five percent, another hundred dollars hidden in their rate. SWIFT and intermediary fees, around sixty. Total leakage, three hundred and sixty dollars and four days. CNY route. One conversion at point four percent, eighty dollars, settled same day. Difference, two hundred and eighty dollars on a single order. Run twelve orders a year and that's three thousand three hundred and sixty dollars sitting in someone else's pocket. WorldFirst's FX is near-interbank on CNY. The math gets honest. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Everyone says pay in USD because it's safer. Truth is, USD is just the most expensive way to send money to China. The dollar isn't a safety feature. It's two FX conversions in a trench coat. Your bank takes a cut going in. The supplier's bank takes a cut coming out. The PI says USD but neither the sender nor the receiver actually lives in USD. The currency exists for about six minutes inside the wire. You're paying two percent for those six minutes. Pay in the currency the supplier actually uses. One conversion. Half the cost. Same destination account. WorldFirst holds CNY natively. Bio.

videos.video_title: Why your supplier secretly wants CNY

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Pattern we see when USD lands in a Chinese supplier's bank account. Three things happen and none of them are visible from your side. One, the bank holds the wire for seven to ten days for clearance. Two, the supplier pays a forex fee, point three to point eight percent, to convert USD into CNY they can actually use. Three, the supplier eats the currency risk if the rate moves between landing and conversion. None of that is your problem on paper. But it shows up on the next quote. Suppliers pad USD prices by two to five percent to absorb all of that. Offer to pay in CNY and that padding has no reason to exist. Ask for the CNY price on the next PI. Watch what they quote. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY direct. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Quick maths on what your supplier loses when you pay in USD. Wire lands at twenty thousand dollars. Bank holds for seven to ten days. Forex fee, point five percent, that's a hundred dollars off the supplier's side. FX risk during the hold window, anywhere from zero to one point five percent depending on the day. Operationally, the supplier is out one hundred and sixty to four hundred and sixty dollars and a week of cash flow on every order. So they price that risk into the next PI. Two to five percent uplift on the USD quote. That's how a twenty thousand dollar order silently becomes twenty thousand five hundred. Pay in CNY. The padding has no job to do. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY same-day. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Everyone thinks paying in USD is a courtesy to the supplier. Truth is, the supplier is silently charging you for the inconvenience. When USD lands in China, the supplier waits a week, pays a forex fee, and carries the currency risk. They don't complain. They just add two to five percent to the next quote. The PI you think you negotiated hard on already has the USD tax baked in. Stop paying for the privilege of using the wrong currency. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY direct. Bio.

videos.video_title: The math on USD vs CNY for a $20,000 supplier payment

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Pattern we see on every twenty thousand dollar supplier payment that goes USD versus CNY. Route A, USD wire. Sender bank's FX margin, one percent, that's two hundred dollars gone. Supplier's bank converts USD to CNY at point five percent on their side, another hundred priced into the next quote. SWIFT and intermediary fees, around sixty dollars across two correspondent hops. Total leakage, three hundred and sixty dollars and four days waiting. Route B, CNY direct. One conversion at point four percent, eighty dollars, settled same day. Difference per order, two hundred and eighty dollars. Twelve orders a year, three thousand three hundred and sixty dollars. Same supplier, same volume, same product. The only thing that changes is the rail. WorldFirst's FX is near-interbank. The math gets honest. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Quick maths on the same twenty thousand dollar order paid two ways. USD route. One percent on the way out, two hundred dollars. Point five percent on the way in, a hundred dollars baked into the next PI. SWIFT fees, sixty dollars across the chain. Total, three hundred and sixty dollars per order. Four days to settle. CNY route. Point four percent total, eighty dollars. Same day. Eighty versus three hundred and sixty. Run twelve orders a year. Two thousand eight hundred dollars in your account instead of theirs. Run an annual sourcing book of two hundred and fifty thousand. Save four thousand five hundred. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY same-day. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Everyone thinks the order cost is the order cost. Truth is, the order cost is the rate plus the rail. On a twenty thousand dollar supplier payment, the rate is what the supplier quoted. The rail is what your bank and their bank silently extract on the way through. USD rail. Three hundred and sixty dollars of leakage. Four days of waiting. CNY rail. Eighty dollars and a same-day arrival. The supplier didn't get more money in either case. You just paid different amounts of toll. Stop debating with the supplier on price. Change the rail. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY direct. Bio.

card_title: The 3-letter code that decides if your wire is same-day

videos.video_title: The 3-letter code on every China wire (most operators never see it)

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay)
variants.vo: Pattern we see on almost every operator's SWIFT wire to China. There's a three-letter code on field seventy-one A of the MT103, and the bank picks it for the operator without asking. Three options. OUR means the sender pays all the intermediary fees, the supplier gets the full amount. SHA means shared, intermediaries deduct from the amount, the supplier gets less. BEN means the beneficiary covers everything, the supplier eats every fee in the chain. Most banks default to SHA because it's cheaper for them and quieter for the customer. The wire goes out, the supplier gets short, and nobody connects the dots back to a three-letter code on a form. Check field seventy-one A on your last wire confirmation. If it says SHA, your supplier just paid the intermediaries. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers without the SWIFT chain. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay)
variants.vo: Quick maths on the three-letter code on every China SWIFT wire. Field seventy-one A. SHA, the default. One to three correspondent banks each deduct fifteen to fifty dollars off the wire before it lands. On a fifty thousand dollar payment, that's up to a hundred and fifty gone, plus the supplier's bank's inbound fee. OUR, the upgrade. Sender pays thirty to seventy-five dollars more upfront, supplier gets the full amount. BEN, the trap. Supplier covers every fee in the chain, can lose two hundred plus on a single wire. Three minutes to change the code. Saves seventy-five dollars on average and three days of supplier chasing. WorldFirst skips field seventy-one A entirely on CNY payments. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay)
variants.vo: Everyone thinks the wire amount is the wire amount. Truth is, three letters on a form decide whether your supplier gets it. Field seventy-one A of the MT103. SHA, OUR, BEN. Three letters. Most banks pick SHA by default because nobody is going to read the form. SHA means the intermediaries take their cut from the wire, not from you. Supplier gets less. Supplier emails you. You blame the bank. The bank blames SWIFT. The three letters did the work. WorldFirst doesn't use SWIFT for CNY. There's no seventy-one A to worry about. Bio.

card_title: Supplier said wire didn't arrive. Bank says it did.

videos.video_title: My bank says sent. My supplier says nothing. Both right.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay)
variants.vo: Pattern we see roughly one in ten wires to China. The bank app says sent. The supplier on WhatsApp says nothing arrived. Both are telling the truth. When a bank says sent, it means the wire left the bank's system. When the supplier says nothing, it means the funds haven't landed in their account. The space between is where the intermediary correspondents sit. Sometimes hours. Sometimes days. Currency conversion windows can add up to two working days on their own. Local Chinese bank holidays cause silent delays that nobody flags. AML and fraud thresholds can hold wires above certain amounts without notifying the sender. The wire isn't lost. It's just somewhere in the chain that neither side can see. Track by SWIFT reference, not by sent. WorldFirst routes around the chain entirely on CNY. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay)
variants.vo: Quick maths on the gap between bank-says-sent and supplier-says-nothing. SWIFT processing window, six to twenty-four hours per correspondent hop. Typical US to China chain, two correspondents, that's twelve to forty-eight hours just on transit. Add currency conversion windows, up to two working days. Add local Chinese bank holidays, anywhere from one to three extra days during golden week or spring festival. Add AML and fraud holds on anything over fifty thousand, another twenty-four to seventy-two hours. Total visible-to-invisible gap can stretch from one to seven business days, and the sender gets no notification at any stage. Get the SWIFT reference number. Use the SWIFT tracker. That's the only real status. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers same-day, no correspondent chain. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: 1688 World Pay)
variants.vo: Everyone thinks sent means arrived. It doesn't. Sent means the wire left your bank. Arrived means it landed in the supplier's account. Three banks live in the middle, plus a currency conversion, plus a local holiday calendar, plus an AML check. Any one of those can hold the wire for two days. All of them together can hold it for a week. Your bank app says sent and stops talking. The supplier says nothing and keeps asking. Both right. The middle is just invisible to both of you. WorldFirst skips the middle. Bio.

card_title: Why your CNY payment sometimes arrives in USD

videos.video_title: 3 places where your CNY can become USD

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Pattern we see on operators trying to pay Chinese suppliers in CNY through standard banks. The wire goes out as CNY. It lands as USD. Supplier confused, operator confused, bank says everything was fine. Three places this can happen. Point one, sender's bank converts CNY to USD before sending because USD is cheaper for them to route. Point two, the intermediary correspondent in the middle of the chain only holds USD, so it converts on the way through. Point three, the receiving bank in China converts because it can't accept CNY directly from a non-Chinese source. Each conversion adds a spread, and the supplier ends up holding USD when they were expecting CNY. Then they convert USD back to CNY at their bank. Double conversion. Double cost. Map every conversion point on your chain and eliminate them. WorldFirst holds CNY natively, that's why this doesn't happen. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Quick maths on why your CNY payment sometimes lands as USD. Three conversion points in a standard cross-border wire. Sender bank, point five to one percent margin to convert your local currency to USD instead of CNY. Correspondent bank, another point three percent if it has to convert mid-flight to settle. Receiving bank, point three to point eight percent to convert USD back to CNY for the supplier. Three conversions on a twenty thousand dollar payment. Stack one percent plus point three percent plus point five percent, that's one point eight percent, three hundred and sixty dollars gone to FX spread alone. Plus the supplier pays the inbound conversion fee on their side. Direct CNY payment uses one conversion at point three to point five percent. Sixty to a hundred dollars total. WorldFirst routes CNY direct, one conversion, no chain. Bio.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst CNY direct (internal: World Account CNY)
variants.vo: Everyone thinks if you send CNY you get CNY. Banks quietly convert mid-flight whenever it's cheaper for them. Three points where it can happen. Sender side, correspondent in the middle, receiving bank on the China end. The wire goes out as CNY and lands as USD because somewhere along the way, a bank decided USD was easier for their books. The supplier ends up holding the wrong currency, converts it back to CNY themselves, and the next PI quietly absorbs the cost. Send the currency the supplier actually receives. WorldFirst holds CNY natively. Bio.
